topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The hammering started just as the sun dipped below the horizon, casting long, skeletal shadows across my yard. I stood by the window, my knuckles white where I gripped the sill, watching the neighbor’s tools move with aggressive efficiency. Each *thwack* of the hammer against the post sounded like a gunshot in the deepening twilight. My breath hitched in my chest, a shallow, rapid thing. I kept my eyes fixed on the street, willing myself to become invisible, to simply cease existing in this moment. The sound of the wood splintering sent a tremor through the floorboards beneath my feet. I wanted to bolt, to disappear into the dark, but my legs felt cemented to the spot, rooted by a paralyzing dread.

[story 2]

"Just a few more posts, Mr. Henderson," the neighbor called out, his voice booming across the quiet cul-de-sac. I retreated inside, pulling the curtains tight against the encroaching darkness. Every time a piece of wire was strung taut between the newly set supports, a cold knot tightened in my stomach. I could hear the scrape of the shovel, the rhythmic grunt of effort, and the unsettling sense of invasion. I started pacing the length of my living room, my gaze darting toward the thin gap in the hedge separating our properties. My palms were slick with a clammy sweat, and I found myself gripping the edge of the armchair so hard my fingers ached. This felt wrong; too much noise, too much presence.

[story 3]

I tried to focus on the pattern of the moonlight on the lawn, anything to distract from the relentless activity next door. The sound of the saw cutting through the air was sharp and jarring. I watched the neighbor wrestle with a length of lumber, his movements jerky and forceful. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my throat feel tight and dry. I wanted to scream, to demand that this construction cease immediately, but no sound escaped. I stood perfectly still, my shoulders hunched up around my ears, trying to shrink my frame against the wall. The sheer scale of the project, the relentless imposition of another person’s will onto my quiet space, made my skin crawl.
